Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Steven Chong JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JCA, Kannan Ramesh JAD and Judith Prakash SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: OP3 International Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: OP3 International Pte Ltd was incorporated in December 2006 as a company in Singapore.
OP3 International Pte Ltd carried on business in interior design, decorating consultancy and construction services.
From incorporation, OP3 International Pte Ltd had Mr Foo Kian Beng as its sole director and shareholder.

On 19 July 2013, OP3 International Pte Ltd entered into a contract with Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd to provide fitting out works at one of Smile Inc's clinics for a contract sum of $158,010, with 50% payable on execution and the balance payable upon completion of the works.

Under the contract dated 19 July 2013 between OP3 International Pte Ltd and Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd, the contractually agreed date of completion of the fitting out works was 11 September 2013.
On or about 31 October 2013, OP3 International Pte Ltd completed the fitting out works at the Smile Inc clinic and handed the clinic over to Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd.
On 9 January 2014, Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd observed mould growing on the walls of the clinic that had been fitted out by OP3 International Pte Ltd.
On 17 January 2024, Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd discovered that the mould was the result of a flood in the clinic and notified OP3 of it on the same day.

Upon learning of the First Flood, OP3 undertook rectification works at the Clinic, including assessing that water from the Clinic’s sinks and spittoons was not draining properly and that the flooding occurred at the Clinic’s drainage sump area.
On 20 February 2014, a contractor engaged by OP3 International Pte Ltd conducted tests at the clinic to check for leakages from the sump and water pipes.
On 21 February 2014, further tests were conducted at the clinic by a contractor engaged by OP3 International Pte Ltd to check for leakages from the sump and water pipes.
On 8 March 2014, OP3 International Pte Ltd handed the clinic back to Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd after carrying out rectification works.
On 21 July 2014, Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd observed mould growth on the walls of the clinic for a second time following another flood originating at the clinic's drainage sump area.
On 22 July 2014, Ms Grace Chong, Managing Director of Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd, informed Mr Foo Kian Beng about the second flood at the clinic.
On 23 July 2014, Mr Foo Kian Beng visited the Smile Inc clinic to look into the circumstances surrounding the second flood.
On 28 and 29 August 2014, Mr Foo engaged a second contractor, AXN Engineering Pte Ltd, to conduct further hydrostatic tests on the Clinic’s water pipes.
On 28 and 29 August 2014, Mr Foo engaged a second contractor, AXN Engineering Pte Ltd, to conduct further hydrostatic tests on the Clinic’s water pipes.
On 15 December 2015, Mr Parwani advised Mr Foo by e-mail that OP3 had a strong defence to Smile Inc’s claim in Suit 498.
On 22 March 2016, OP3 engaged an independent expert, Mr Chee Yan Pong, to provide his opinion as to the cause of the First and Second Floods.
Mr Chee prepared a report dated 21 September 2016 setting out his views on the cause of the First and Second Floods.
On 10 December 2015, the director’s resolution authorised payment of a dividend of $1,200,000 to Mr Foo.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid a dividend of $1,200,000 to Mr Foo Kian Beng pursuant to the 10 December 2015 director's resolution.
On 10 December 2015, the director’s resolution authorised payment of a dividend of $700,000 to Mr Foo.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid a dividend of $700,000 to Mr Foo Kian Beng pursuant to the 10 December 2015 director's resolution.
On 8 July 2016, the director’s resolution authorised payment of a dividend of $400,000 to Mr Foo.
On 8 July 2016, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Mr Foo Kian Beng signed a resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $400,000 to himself.
On 2017, OP3 paid Mr Foo $682,394 as repayment of moneys he had earlier loaned to the company.

On 10 February 2021, the liquidator of OP3 International Pte Ltd commenced HC/S 152/2021 in the name of OP3 International Pte Ltd against Mr Foo Kian Beng seeking to recover sums equivalent to payments made to him between 2015 and 2017.

On 20 January 2022, lawyer Vijai Parwani issued a letter stating that he had previously advised Mr Foo Kian Beng on 22 August 2014 and 25 May 2015 that Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498 was unmeritorious.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether payments of dividends and loan repayments from OP3 International Pte Ltd to its sole director and shareholder during the period when the company faced a substantial claim from Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd should be reversed on the basis that they were made at times when the company’s financial position required its decision-maker to take into account the interests of creditors.
Party_Details.Rule: A company director must act in what the director considers to be the best interests of the company. When the company is approaching a state where it is likely to be unable to discharge its debts, the director’s assessment of the company’s interests includes having regard to the position of creditors and the effect of proposed transactions on the company’s ability to meet its obligations.
Party_Details.Application: OP3 International Pte Ltd entered into a contract in July 2013 to perform fitting out works for Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd and later faced a claim exceeding the original contract sum after two flooding incidents at the clinic. Smile Inc sent a letter of demand in August 2014 and served a writ and statement of claim in May 2015, and OP3 obtained legal and expert input about the dispute. During the period from December 2015 to 2017, while the Smile Inc claim remained unresolved, OP3 International Pte Ltd, acting through its sole director and shareholder, authorised and made multiple large dividend payments and loan repayments to that same individual. These transactions reduced the funds remaining in the company while a significant unresolved claim from a customer was outstanding and before the company was later wound up on that claim.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal analysed the nature, scope and timing of the so‑called Creditor Duty as an aspect of the director’s fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the company, drawing extensively on local authorities and the UK Supreme Court decision in BTI 2014 LLC v Sequana SA. The court rejected a reconceptualisation of the duty as existing regardless of solvency and maintained a two‑stage inquiry: first, objectively assessing the company’s financial state at the time of the impugned transaction (including the effect of the transaction itself), and second, examining whether the director subjectively acted in what he considered to be the company’s best interests, tested against that financial context. The court identified three categories of financial state: (1) solvency, where shareholder interests can generally be treated as a proxy for company interests; (2) an intermediate zone where the company is imminently likely to be unable to discharge its debts, in which directors must give increasing weight to creditors’ interests and transactions that primarily benefit shareholders, such as dividends and shareholder loan repayments, attract heightened scrutiny; and (3) a stage where insolvency proceedings are inevitable, at which point creditors are effectively the main economic stakeholders and transactions that exclusively benefit shareholders or directors at the expense of creditors are inconsistent with the duty. Applying this framework, the court accepted that OP3’s potential liability to Smile Inc in Suit 498 constituted a contingent liability that became reasonably likely to materialise at least by the time the statement of claim was served in May 2015. The court considered the quantum of that contingent liability by reference to the pleaded claim, adjustments for conceded weak components, and reasonable discounts for litigation risk and overstatement, and concluded that when this contingent liability was factored into OP3’s position, the company was balance sheet insolvent by the end of 2016 and 2017. The court agreed with the High Court that earlier payments in 2015 and the July 2016 dividend were made when OP3 was not yet in a state that engaged the Creditor Duty to the same extent, but that by December 2016 and during 2017 the company had entered at least the intermediate category, if not worse, such that the director was required to give substantial weight to creditors’ interests. The court examined the legal advice and other material relied on by Mr Foo, including the 2015 email from his litigation counsel, the later 2022 letter, the expert report on the cause of the flooding, and informal input from an auditor, and concluded that these did not justify treating the Smile Inc claim as unlikely to materialise or negligible. The court noted that the contemporaneous legal advice was sparse and undetailed, that the later letter was hearsay in the High Court and in any event post‑dated the events, that the expert report addressed technical causation rather than the prudence of extracting funds from the company, and that the auditor’s informal view was based on an assumption that no provision for the contingent liability was needed. The court also found that the Disputed Dividend recorded as of 27 December 2016 and the 2017 payment of $682,394 were distinct transactions, rejecting Mr Foo’s contention that the dividend was subsumed within the later loan repayment, based on the accounting records and the pattern of payments. In light of OP3’s financial state at the end of 2016 and in 2017, the court held that authorising the Disputed Dividend and the 2017 loan repayment to the sole shareholder and director, while a substantial unresolved claim from Smile Inc remained and the company’s assets were insufficient to meet that exposure, did not reflect proper regard for creditors’ interests. The court inferred from the nature and timing of the transactions, and the absence of a legitimate corporate purpose beyond benefiting the shareholder, that Mr Foo had not complied with the Creditor Duty in relation to those payments. The court rejected Mr Foo’s argument that OP3’s claim was in substance an attempt to enforce section 403 of the Companies Act and therefore beyond the company’s standing, explaining that the statutory rule on payment of dividends out of profits and the Creditor Duty are distinct though overlapping regimes, with different addressees, measures of liability and conceptions of profits. It also rejected the contention that the expiry of the statutory clawback period for unfair preferences barred a fiduciary duty claim, pointing to regulation 9 of the Companies (Application of Bankruptcy Provisions) Regulations and prior authority recognising that avoidance provisions operate without prejudice to other remedies, including claims for breach of duty. On relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, the court held that Mr Foo had not shown that he acted both honestly and reasonably in authorising the Disputed Transactions, emphasising that he had not sought specific advice on the propriety of extracting large sums from OP3 in light of the Smile Inc claim and the company’s financial position, and that the pattern and scale of the payments were inconsistent with a reasonable appreciation of creditor risk. The Court of Appeal therefore upheld the High Court’s order requiring Mr Foo to repay the amounts corresponding to the Disputed Dividend and the 2017 loan repayment and dismissed the appeal.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Steven Chong JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JCA, Kannan Ramesh JAD and Judith Prakash SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Foo Kian Beng

Facts.Text: From the time of incorporation of OP3 International Pte Ltd in December 2006, Foo Kian Beng was its sole director and sole shareholder.
As sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng oversaw completion of the fitting out works at the Smile Inc clinic and the handover on or about 31 October 2013.

After Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd notified OP3 International Pte Ltd on 17 January 2014 of a flood and mould at the clinic, Foo Kian Beng arranged for OP3 to carry out rectification works, including installing an access panel near the drainage sump area and engaging a contractor to check for leaks.
On 23 July 2014, after being informed of a second flood at the clinic, Foo Kian Beng went to the clinic to look into the situation.
Following the second flood, Foo Kian Beng engaged AXN Engineering Pte Ltd to conduct hydrostatic tests on the clinic's water pipes on 28 and 29 August 2014.

On 15 December 2015, lawyer Vijai Parwani of Parwani Law LLC emailed Foo Kian Beng stating that OP3 International Pte Ltd had a strong defence to Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498.
On 22 March 2016, acting for OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng engaged expert Mr Chee Yan Pong to provide an opinion on the causes of the flooding incidents at the Smile Inc clinic.

On 21 September 2016, expert Mr Chee Yan Pong delivered a report to OP3 International Pte Ltd, addressed to its management including Foo Kian Beng, setting out his views on the causes of the first and second floods.
On 22 August 2014 and 25 May 2015, according to a later letter, lawyer Vijai Parwani communicated advice to Foo Kian Beng that Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498 was unmeritorious.
On 10 December 2015, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng signed a resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $1,200,000 to himself.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $1,200,000 to Foo Kian Beng as a dividend pursuant to the 10 December 2015 resolution.
On 10 December 2015, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng signed a further resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $700,000 to himself.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $700,000 to Foo Kian Beng as a dividend pursuant to the 10 December 2015 resolution.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $138,352 to Foo Kian Beng as repayment of money he had previously loaned to the company.
On 8 July 2016, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng signed a resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $400,000 to himself.
On 13 July 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $400,000 to Foo Kian Beng as a dividend.
On 27 December 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd recorded a director's resolution for a dividend of $500,000 in favour of Foo Kian Beng.
In 2017, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid a total of $682,394 to Foo Kian Beng as repayment of money he had previously loaned to the company.

On 20 January 2022, lawyer Vijai Parwani issued a letter stating that he had advised Foo Kian Beng on 22 August 2014 and 25 May 2015 that Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498 was unmeritorious.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, at the times when he authorised dividends and loan repayments from OP3 International Pte Ltd to himself while the company faced a substantial unresolved claim from Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng was take into account the position of OP3’s creditors and whether the payments should be reversed.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who controls a company must act in what he considers to be the best interests of the company. When the company is in a financial position where it is imminently likely to be unable to discharge its debts, or where insolvency proceedings are inevitable, the director’s assessment of the company’s interests must include consideration of the impact of proposed transactions on creditors as a group and the weight given to creditors’ interests increases with the severity of the company’s financial difficulties.
Party_Details.Application: Foo Kian Beng, as sole director and shareholder of OP3 International Pte Ltd, controlled decisions on payments from the company. After Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd sent a letter of demand in August 2014 and served a writ and statement of claim in May 2015 for more than $1.5 million, OP3 faced a significant contingent liability arising from the clinic project. During the period from December 2015 through 2017, while that claim remained unresolved, Foo Kian Beng caused OP3 to pay him multiple large dividends and to repay loans he had made to the company, including resolutions and payments in December 2015, July 2016, a recorded dividend resolution in December 2016, and a substantial loan repayment in 2017. He obtained some legal and accounting input, including an email from litigation counsel describing OP3’s defence as strong, a later letter describing earlier advice, an expert report on the cause of the flooding, and informal confirmation from an auditor that payments were permissible. The court evaluated these materials and the company’s financial information and concluded that, by the end of 2016 and in 2017, when the disputed dividend and loan repayment were made or recorded, OP3’s financial position, once the contingent liability from Suit 498 was taken into account, had moved into a state where the company was at least imminently likely to be unable to discharge its debts. In that context, the pattern of payments to the sole shareholder and director, and the absence of a corporate purpose beyond transferring value to him, led the court to determine that he had not given appropriate weight to creditors’ interests when authorising the disputed transactions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal treated Foo Kian Beng as the controlling mind of OP3 International Pte Ltd and examined his conduct in light of the Creditor Duty framework it articulated. The court first rejected his new standing argument that OP3’s claim was effectively an attempt to enforce section 403 of the Companies Act, holding that the statutory rule on dividends and the fiduciary duty to consider creditors’ interests are distinct, overlapping regimes. It also rejected his contention that the expiry of the statutory clawback period for unfair preferences barred a fiduciary duty claim, relying on regulation 9 of the Companies (Application of Bankruptcy Provisions) Regulations and prior authority confirming that avoidance provisions operate without prejudice to other remedies. On the temporal scope of the Creditor Duty, the court declined to adopt his position that the duty arises only when a company is insolvent or on the verge of insolvency, and instead endorsed a three‑category model: solvency, an intermediate zone of imminent inability to pay debts, and inevitability of insolvency proceedings. The court held that in the intermediate zone directors must balance shareholder and creditor interests, with increasing weight to creditors as financial distress deepens, and that transactions such as dividends and shareholder loan repayments in this zone attract heightened scrutiny because they primarily benefit shareholders. Applying this to OP3, the court accepted that the Smile Inc claim created a contingent liability that became reasonably likely to materialise by the time the statement of claim was served in May 2015. It assessed the value of that contingent liability by starting from the pleaded amount, deducting components conceded to be weak, and applying reasonable discounts for litigation risk and overstatement, arriving at a range that, when factored into OP3’s accounts, rendered the company balance sheet insolvent by the end of 2016 and 2017. The court distinguished between earlier payments in 2015 and the July 2016 dividend, which it accepted were made before the company entered the more acute stages of financial distress, and the later Disputed Dividend and 2017 loan repayment, which occurred when OP3 was already in the intermediate or worse category. The court examined the legal and professional input on which Foo relied. It found that the 2015 email from litigation counsel, which simply described OP3’s defence as strong, lacked detail and context and did not justify treating the claim as unlikely to succeed. The 2022 letter recounting earlier advice was treated as hearsay in the High Court and, in any event, as a retrospective document that did not alter the contemporaneous risk profile. The expert report by Mr Chee was directed at technical causation issues in the underlying construction dispute and did not address the prudence of extracting funds from OP3 while the claim was pending. The informal view of auditor Wong King Kheng was based on an assumption that no provision for the contingent liability was needed and was given outside a formal engagement. The court concluded that these materials did not reasonably support Foo’s position that the Smile Inc claim could be ignored for solvency assessment. On the factual dispute about whether the $500,000 dividend recorded as of 27 December 2016 was a separate payment or part of the 2017 loan repayment, the court upheld the High Court’s finding that they were distinct transactions, relying on the company’s financial statements and the pattern of entries. Considering OP3’s financial state at the end of 2016 and in 2017, the court held that authorising the Disputed Dividend and the 2017 loan repayment to himself, in circumstances where OP3’s assets were insufficient to cover the reasonably valued contingent liability to Smile Inc, exposed creditors to increased risk and did not reflect proper regard for their interests. The court inferred from the nature, timing and beneficiaries of the transactions that Foo had not acted in what he considered to be the company’s best interests in the sense required when creditors’ interests had come to the fore. On Foo’s request for relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, the court held that he had not demonstrated that he acted both honestly and reasonably. It emphasised that he had not sought specific advice on the propriety of making large distributions to himself in light of OP3’s financial position and the outstanding claim, and that the scale and pattern of the payments were inconsistent with a reasonable appreciation of creditor risk. The court therefore affirmed the High Court’s conclusion that Foo was responsible to OP3 for the amounts corresponding to the Disputed Dividend and the 2017 loan repayment and dismissed his appeal.
